M3 · Multivariate SUR covariance

JoonHo Lee

2026-07-12

Source: vignettes/m3-multivariate-sur-covariance.Rmd
m3-multivariate-sur-covariance.Rmd

Abstract

For methodologists working on Scenario B (overlapping binary indicators with SUR-style covariance). Derives the cross-indicator covariance matrix, locks the sm_vcov class spec, and runs the PSD invariant on alprek_subset.

Overview

This article is written for methodologists working on Scenario B — overlapping binary indicators whose within-site cross-covariance is non-zero — who need the exact covariance matrix sitemix assembles and why it takes this form. We cover, in order:

  1. the joint within-site structure an analyst sees and what sitemix encodes;
  2. the local notation this derivation adds;
  3. the SUR cross-covariance derivation and its finite-population corrections;
  4. the sm_vcov class spec;
  5. PSD validation of the assembled matrix;
  6. the implementation invariants.

Established vs. novel. Established: the seemingly-unrelated-regression cross-covariance structure (Zellner, 1962) and design-based finite-population corrections under SRSWOR. This package: the sm_vcov object contract — scale/method provenance, PSD validation, and the diagonal exceptions — that packages these results for site-year panels.

Result Attribution
SUR cross-covariance structure Zellner (1962)
SRSWOR finite-population correction standard design-based sampling
sm_vcov object contract & PSD validation this package (sitemix)

About the example data. All results here are computed live from alprek_subset, an anonymized, illustrative 50-site sample of Alabama First Class Pre-K records shipped in the package (see ?alprek_subset). It is not a real accountability dataset and must not be cited as empirical Pre-K results. Every code block runs offline with a fixed random seed.

1. What an analyst sees, what sitemix encodes

For Scenario B with KK overlapping binary indicators (e.g., FRPM, SNAP, WIC, TANF), the within-site joint distribution is not a simple product of binomials — students can be in multiple indicators simultaneously, so the per-row covariance across indicators is non-zero. sm_estimate(family = "multivariate", vjt = TRUE) returns a V list-column whose jj-th element is a K×KK \times Ksm_vcov object.

2. Notation map (extends M2)

Symbol Meaning Code Range
KK Indicator-component count length(indicators) ≥2\ge 2
Cjt,kk′C_{jt,kk'} Joint count (both kk and k′k' positive) (intermediate) ≥0\ge 0
pxyp_{xy} Joint proportion estimate (intermediate) [0,min⁡(px,py)][0, \min(p_x, p_y)]
σkk′\sigma_{kk'} Cross-covariance element V[[i]]$matrix[k,k'] symmetric
Σjt\Sigma_{jt} Assembled K×KK \times K matrix V[[i]]$matrix PSD up to 1e-12

3. The SUR cross-covariance derivation

Theorem 1 (population SUR cross-covariance). For two overlapping binary indicators XkX_k and Xk′X_{k'} measured on the same nn IID students per site-year,

Cov⁡(π̂k,π̂k′)=πkk′−πkπk′njt, \boxed{ \operatorname{Cov}(\hat\pi_k,\hat\pi_{k'}) \;=\; \frac{\pi_{kk'} - \pi_k\pi_{k'}}{n_{jt}}, } \tag{M3.1}

where πk=Pr⁡(Xk=1)\pi_k=\Pr(X_k=1), πk′=Pr⁡(Xk′=1)\pi_{k'}=\Pr(X_{k'}=1), and πkk′=Pr⁡(Xk=1,Xk′=1)\pi_{kk'}=\Pr(X_k=1,X_{k'}=1) are population probabilities.

Proof sketch. Treat each student as a Bernoulli trial in 2K2^K-cell multinomial space. The sample covariance of the two binary marginals reduces to Cov(Xk,Xk′)/n\mathrm{Cov}(X_k, X_{k'})/n, which simplifies as above. ▫\square

Attribution. Standard SUR result; see Zellner (1962).

The package does not observe the population probabilities in (M3.1). It substitutes the empirical proportions. If Q=∑i(yi−y‾)(yi−y‾)⊤Q=\sum_i(y_i-\bar y)(y_i-\bar y)^\top, the default no-FPC matrix is the plug-in estimate V̂plugin=Q/n2\widehat V_{\mathrm{plugin}}=Q/n^2; the binomial_bc alternative is Q/[n(n−1)]Q/[n(n-1)]. Its diagonal therefore recovers the corresponding estimated Scenario A raw-scale variance, not the unknown population variance itself. This raw-scale matrix convention is the one used for Scenario B in sitemix (Lee, 2026).

For a fixed population of size NN sampled without replacement, define

q={0,N=n,(N−n)/(N−1),N>n. q=\begin{cases} 0, & N=n,\\ (N-n)/(N-1), & N>n. \end{cases}

The first branch includes the exact one-unit census N=n=1N=n=1. The approved SRSWOR whole-matrix rules are

V̂plugin=qQ/n2,V̂design={0K×K,N=n,(N−n)Q/[Nn(n−1)],N>n and n>1. \widehat V_{\mathrm{plugin}}=qQ/n^2,\qquad \widehat V_{\mathrm{design}}= \begin{cases} 0_{K\times K}, & N=n,\\ (N-n)Q/[Nn(n-1)], & N>n\text{ and }n>1. \end{cases} \tag{M3.2}

These are standard without-replacement sampling corrections (Cochran, 1977).

The second rule is selected by bias_correction = “binomial_bc”. Both scale the entire matrix rather than replacing only its diagonal, so symmetry, PSD, and correlation structure are preserved. N = n is a valid census and produces a zero matrix. A Wilson boundary surrogate remains a named scalar/diagonal regularizer and carries the conventional qq multiplier.

The four implemented interior-cell cases are therefore:

Variance rule Infinite-population / no supplied NN Fixed-population SRSWOR
plug-in Q/n2Q/n^2 qQ/n2qQ/n^2
binomial_bc Q/[n(n−1)]Q/[n(n-1)] (N−n)Q/[Nn(n−1)](N-n)Q/[Nn(n-1)]

The same multiplier applies to every matrix entry. A diagonal-only correction would change correlations and is not the implemented estimand. For a constant indicator, empirical cross-covariances are exactly zero. boundary_method = "wilson_floor" may replace that indicator’s zero diagonal with a positive, explicitly named scalar/diagonal surrogate, but it never creates off-diagonal covariance. Agresti–Coull boundary regularization is illegal with vjt = TRUE.

4. The sm_vcov class spec (canonical home)

This vignette is the method location for the sm_vcov class spec. The primary fields used by Scenario B are:

  • matrix — the K×KK \times K symmetric PSD numeric matrix.
  • vcov_scale — one of "raw", "arcsine_delta", "logit_delta", or reserved "reference_raw". Under Scenario B’s SUR construction, vcov_scale = "raw" regardless of the row’s estimate_scale (the SUR formula is numerically stable in raw space).
  • vcov_method — set to "sur" for Scenario B output.
  • indicator_order and matrix_rank — record the component order and numerical rank.
  • Finite-population provenance — population size, coordinate-aligned sampling fraction, conventional FPC, actually applied multiplier, sampling design, and variance rule. These fields do not alter n_eff.
  • Diagonal contract — “row_se_raw_squared” for Scenario B, making explicit that the raw matrix diagonal matches se_raw squared, not transformed se squared.

For a package-produced Scenario B sitemix_estimates object, validation cross-checks those matrix fields against every row, including the site-year, estimate scale, common denominator, scalar correction rule, and boundary rule. Repeating the same tampered V over all group rows therefore cannot bypass the contract. A stand-alone user-constructed sm_vcov remains a more general carrier until it is attached to package output.

The full lexicons are documented at ?sm_vcov: vcov_method is NA, "sur", "multinomial", or "working_independence"; vcov_scale is "raw", "arcsine_delta", "logit_delta", or "reference_raw".

5. PSD validation

The assembled Σjt\Sigma_{jt} is PSD by construction for non-degenerate joint counts. Numerical roundoff can produce a smallest eigenvalue slightly below zero; the constructor validates PSD-ness up to the documented tolerance. The Scenario B path records psd_repair = "none" because no repair is applied.

6. Implementation invariants

ID Layer Claim
SUR1 engine-multivariate (M3.1) holds exactly for two indicators sharing the same nn.
SUR2 sm_vcov Every assembled V[[i]]$matrix satisfies the documented scale-aware PSD tolerance.
SUR3 sm_vcov vcov_method = "sur" and vcov_scale = "raw" for Scenario B.
SUR4 correction Plug-in and binomial_bc scale the whole matrix, including off-diagonals.
SUR5 diagonal contract diag(V) = se_raw^2; transformed row se^2 is intentionally different.
SUR6 boundary Constant-indicator off-diagonals are zero; Wilson is a named diagonal surrogate.

Verify on alprek_subset:

data(alprek_subset, package = "sitemix")
est <- sm_estimate(
  subset(alprek_subset, year == 2024),
  family = "multivariate",
  indicators = c("frpm", "snap"),
  vjt = TRUE
)
V1 <- as.matrix(est$V[[1L]])

# SUR1: closed form cross-covariance
slice <- subset(alprek_subset, year == 2024 & site_id == est$site_id[1L])
n <- nrow(slice)
p_x <- mean(slice$frpm)
p_y <- mean(slice$snap)
p_xy <- mean(slice$frpm * slice$snap)
manual_sigma <- (p_xy - p_x * p_y) / n
stopifnot(all.equal(V1[1, 2], manual_sigma, tolerance = 1e-10))

# SUR2: PSD
eig_V1 <- eigen(V1, symmetric = TRUE, only.values = TRUE)$values
tol_V1 <- 64 * nrow(V1) * .Machine$double.eps * max(abs(eig_V1)) +
  64 * nrow(V1) * .Machine$double.xmin
stopifnot(min(eig_V1) >= -tol_V1)

# SUR3: lexicon
stopifnot(est$V[[1L]]$vcov_method == "sur")
stopifnot(est$V[[1L]]$vcov_scale == "raw")
stopifnot(est$V[[1L]]$diag_contract == "row_se_raw_squared")
stopifnot(est$estimate_scale[[1L]] == "arcsine")
stopifnot(est$V[[1L]]$psd_repair == "none")

The three checked invariants (SUR1–SUR3) hold (algebraic tolerance 1e-10; PSD uses the scale-aware relative plus machine-range rule above). SUR4–SUR6 in the table above are stated but not checked in this chunk.

7. Where to go next

  • M4 · Multinomial simplex — the sister derivation for Scenario C (mutually exclusive categories).
  • M5 · Aggregate engines D0 / D1 for the D1 working-independence variant of VV.
  • A4 · Scenarios B / C — multivariate / multinomial for the applied face of (M3.1) and the vcov_scale = "raw" convention.

References

Cochran, W. G. (1977). Sampling techniques (3rd ed.). John Wiley & Sons.
Lee, J. (2026). sitemix: Site- and group-level proportions, rates, and sampling uncertainty. https://joonho112.github.io/sitemix/
Zellner, A. (1962). An efficient method of estimating seemingly unrelated regressions and tests for aggregation bias. Journal of the American Statistical Association, 57(298), 348–368. https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.1962.10480664
